text: ABSTRACT Fieldwork is a valuable method for the study of political conflict; however, the detailed procedures of the Institutional Review Board (IRB) appear to present such an overwhelming barrier to the execution of this type of research that it actually deters it. Several factors are pertinent to this matter of fieldwork research in the context of foreign conflict. These are first, that the federal rules regarding ethical standards for research on the human subjects of social science research are essentially designed for use in biomedical research. Second, there is a large gap between “procedural ethics” and “ethics in practice.” Third, there is little communication between researchers and IRBs and therefore little feedback to guide the further improvement of IRB review procedures. Suggestions for improving the research experience include: dialogue between the researcher and the IRB; speedier processing of applications and of subsequent reviews and revisions of research designs; interaction among researchers that can clarify the nature of the research; and that the academic community as a whole play a more activist role.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
